paper.title: Beyond Static Leaderboards: Predictive Validity for the Evaluation of LLM Agents

research_equation.problem: Aggregate-score leaderboards systematically underspecify deployed-agent evaluation, leading to rank instability (e.g., public→hidden Spearman ρ=−0.13 for execution).
research_equation.proposed_solution: Replace in-sample mean ranking with predictive validity (in-sample vs out-of-sample rank correlation), operationalized through three OOD criteria and a twelve-tier measurement apparatus.
research_equation.equation: PV(c) = α·Ȳ_c − β·σ_{Y_c,OOD} − γ·IQR(Y_c); rank by PV instead of Ȳ
research_equation.null_hypothesis: In-sample aggregate scores predict out-of-sample rankings with ρ ≥ 0.85 across all OOD criteria; the twelve-tier apparatus collapses to fewer than 12 orthogonal dimensions.

pass_1_what.question: Do aggregate-score leaderboards produce rankings that transfer to deployment conditions?
pass_1_what.answer: No — public-to-hidden competition data shows near-zero correlation on execution (ρ=−0.13) and marginal correlation on planning (ρ=0.69).

pass_2_how.method: Synthesis of 14 parallel implementation studies + 7 prior benchmarks → 12-tier measurement apparatus + 3 OOD criteria + PV score formula
pass_2_how.scope_note: Position paper grounded in synthesis; no new controlled experiments. All evidence from industrial asset O&M domain (AssetOpsBench).
pass_2_how.evidence_structure: Three kinds: (1) 7 benchmark/distilled metrics papers, (2) 14 implementation studies with 6 extension axes, (3) cross-paper triangulation

pass_3_why_now.paradigm_shift: LLM agents have evolved beyond what single-shot benchmarks can evaluate (planning, tool-calling, multi-turn artifact reuse, orchestration diversity)
pass_3_why_now.existing_gap: HELM & Dynabench address multi-dimensionality for single-shot models but miss agent-specific axes; recent calls for 'interactive evaluation as design science' name the gap without proposing a ranking criterion
pass_3_why_now.why_convergent: 14 independent teams modifying the same MCP-based benchmark all surface complementary failure modes the headline metric does not see — this is architectural sensitivity, not isolated findings

challenge_module_map.module: Three OOD criteria (A: held-out, B: cross-subset, C: adversarial)
challenge_module_map.mechanism: Test whether in-sample rank predicts out-of-sample rank across increasingly challenging shifts
Expose dimensions current leaderboards collapse: T8 (infra), T9 (multi-turn), T10 (reasoning), T11 (knowledge), T12 (evidence grounding)
Deterministic verifiers (DAG oracle, rule-based CAR pipeline) as external anchor against judge drift
Submissions declare architecture, reasoning mode, retrieval strategy, prompt-constraint level, verifier type

challenge_module_map.module: PV score with cross-OOD variance penalty
challenge_module_map.mechanism: α·Ȳ − β·σ_OOD − γ·IQR penalizes configurations that are good on mean but unstable across OOD shifts
Latency decomposition isolates MCP-stdio subprocess overhead from reasoning ability

challenge_module_map.challenge: Scenario corridor too small for extension
challenge_module_map.module: Automated scenario generation + community living benchmark (Cluster IV)
challenge_module_map.mechanism: Expand beyond 141-scenario corpus through automated tools (G8) and SME-authored scenarios (G30)

key_insights: Aggregate scores are not wrong — they are underspecified. A Pass1=0.75 can come from fundamentally different architectures with different deployment properties.
The fourteen implementation studies converged on measurement gaps: four independent teams identified MCP-stdio overhead as the dominant latency floor (Convergence Pattern C).
Judge-independent verification is feasible and necessary: ARE's DAG oracle achieves 0.99 precision; LLM judges achieve only α=0.61 Krippendorff.
Cross-subset transfer is the most realistic OOD test: 'you ranked agents on chillers; will the ranking transfer to hydraulic pumps?'
The field needs a different relationship between leaderboards and deployment, not a new benchmark. The four proposals in Section 6 are structural changes to existing leaderboard infrastructure.
QLoRA fine-tuning's catastrophic forgetting (Gemma 79.8% vs Qwen3 61.3% retention) shows that weight-modifying optimizations need longitudinal retention sub-metrics.
